meta.description: Intimacy building positions for all couples. Premium-only with weekly releases.
meta.releaseSchedule: 10 positions at launch (releaseWeek 0), then 1 new position per week through week 190. Full library screens can show all accessible positions for premium users.

items.title: The Tether
items.focus: A grounded, face-to-face lap straddle that feels anchored, mutual, and emotionally close. One partner stays rooted and supportive while the other settles in above them, creating full-body contact through the chest, torso, and hips. The shape invites eye contact, wrapped arms, and a slower rhythm that feels intimate instead of rushed.
items.howTo: One partner sits upright with a stable base while the other straddles them face-to-face, settling into their lap with the legs wrapped around the waist or hips. The seated partner supports from underneath at the hips, waist, or seat, while the top partner can rest their arms around the shoulders or upper back for closeness and balance. Start by finding a comfortable seat and letting both bodies settle before adding movement. This position usually works best with slow rocking, circling, or steady pressure rather than fast motion.
items.benefits: Because both bodies stay stacked and close, this position often feels deeply connected and emotionally present. The upright posture keeps the chest open and makes it easier to stay visually and physically tuned in to each other. The support underneath can make the partner on top feel securely held, while the partner below stays actively involved and connected through the whole body.
items.makeItHotter: Bring the bodies even closer so the chest and stomach stay fully connected. A slower rhythm often makes this one feel stronger, especially when paired with eye contact, kissing, or lingering pauses. Changing how upright or relaxed the top partner is can also shift the feeling a lot, from soft and affectionate to more charged and intense.
items.comfort: This position is usually most comfortable when the seated partner has enough back, hip, and leg support to stay relaxed. Sitting against a headboard, couch arm, wall, or sturdy cushion can help a lot. If the hips feel tight, loosen the leg wrap slightly or place a pillow underneath for height and support. The goal is softness and stability, not strain.
items.bestFor: Deep face-to-face closeness, emotional presence, slow full-body rhythm, feeling anchored together
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels grounded, mutual, and emotionally close. Many people like it because the chest-to-chest contact and face-to-face alignment make it easier to stay present together than in positions where the bodies are angled away from each other.
lessIdealIf: Hip flexibility is limited
The seated partner needs full back support
You want faster or more athletic movement
setupTips: Sit against a headboard or wall for back support
Place a pillow under the hips if needed
Let both bodies settle before building movement
Start with stillness and slow rocking
Keep the leg wrap relaxed, not tense
items.shortSummary: A face-to-face seated straddle that feels grounded, emotionally close, and easy to stay inside.

items.title: The Mirror
items.focus: A deeply intimate, face-to-face seated position built around closeness rather than distance or speed. With both partners upright and gathered into each other, it feels emotionally intense, grounded, and connected — the kind of position that invites eye contact, breath, and staying present.
items.howTo: One partner sits upright while the other settles into their lap facing them, legs wrapping in close so both bodies stay aligned through the chest and hips. The position works best when neither person rushes it — start by finding a comfortable seat, softening into each other, and letting the closeness guide the rhythm. It can be still and embracing or gently rocking, depending on what feels natural.
items.benefits: Because the bodies stay closely stacked and face-to-face, the sensation is often less about intensity from motion and more about emotional connection, pressure, and shared rhythm. Small movements can feel surprisingly powerful here. It tends to feel immersive, affectionate, and very mutual.
items.makeItHotter: Lean in for more chest-to-chest contact, wrap the arms fully around each other, or slow the pace until it becomes more about breathing and subtle motion than thrusting. Eye contact can make this one feel especially vulnerable and intense. A pillow under the seated partner can also help lift the hips and improve comfort.
items.comfort: Hip openness and lower-back support matter here. If the seated position starts to feel tiring, sit against a headboard, wall, or sturdy cushion so the body can relax into it more easily. This one usually feels best when both partners are supported enough to stay soft.
items.bestFor: Quiet emotional intimacy, face-to-face alignment, slow mutual rhythm, staying fully present
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels unusually close and equal. Many people like it because the face-to-face setup invites real eye contact and breath-sharing, and because the position rewards slowing down rather than speeding up.
lessIdealIf: You want faster pace or more range
Hip openness is limited
The mood is more playful than tender
setupTips: Sit against a headboard or wall
Place a pillow under either partner if needed
Start still and let the rhythm emerge
Stay soft in the hips and lower back
Use a slow rocking motion rather than thrusting
items.shortSummary: A deeply intimate face-to-face seated position built around closeness, breath, and slow mutual rhythm.

items.title: The Descent
items.focus: A supported, high-control position where the partner on top stays open, elevated, and in charge of the rhythm. The braced foot placement creates leverage, while the open posture allows for confidence, freedom of movement, and easy touch. It feels powerful, exposed, and highly responsive.
items.howTo: One partner lies flat while the other settles above them in a supported straddle, placing their feet against the lower partner's chest for lift and leverage. The partner on top can brace with their hands on the thighs, bed, floor, or nearby surface to steady themselves and shape the angle. The position works best with slow setup and small adjustments through the hips and torso before building rhythm.
items.benefits: The leverage from the feet and hands gives the top partner more control over pressure, pacing, and angle than a more passive riding position. Because the body stays open, it also allows easier access for clitoral or penile touch, which can make the experience feel more direct and customizable. The result is active, confident, and intensely embodied.
items.makeItHotter: Shift the hands between the thighs and the surface beneath you to see which version gives the best control. A more upright posture can feel bolder and more open, while a slight lean can create a stronger sense of pressure and grounding. This is a position where tiny changes can make a big difference.
items.comfort: If the foot placement on the chest feels too intense, lower it slightly or reduce pressure until it feels stable. The partner underneath may want a pillow beneath the head for support, and the top partner should keep some softness in the knees and ankles rather than locking the joints.
items.bestFor: Top-partner control, open leverage, direct angle, confident self-directed movement
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It gives the partner on top a lot of physical agency. Many people like it because the braced foot placement creates real leverage and because the open posture makes touch and adjustment easy throughout.
lessIdealIf: Ankle or knee sensitivity is present
The partner underneath prefers to lead
You want a slower or more emotionally tender mood
setupTips: Plant the feet steadily before building pace
Brace the hands on the thighs or surface for balance
Start with small hip movements to find the angle
Keep softness in the knees and ankles
Adjust posture before adjusting speed
items.shortSummary: A braced top-partner position with leverage, openness, and strong directional control.

items.title: The Summit
items.focus: A classic rear-entry position that feels grounded, direct, and highly adaptable. One partner is supported on hands and knees or forearms and knees while the other kneels close behind, creating a shape that can feel steady, intense, and easy to control. It can be slow and connected or more energetic, depending on the pace.
items.howTo: One partner comes onto hands and knees or forearms and knees while the other kneels behind them, staying close at the hips to guide the angle and rhythm. The receiving partner can keep the back neutral or add a slight arch, depending on what feels best. This position works well because it is easy to adjust: widen the knees, lower to the forearms, or shift the hips slightly to change comfort and sensation.
items.benefits: Because the bodies are aligned from behind, this position can create a strong, direct angle while still feeling stable. The partner in back can guide pace and depth easily, while the receiving partner can change the feel a lot just by adjusting hip position, leg width, or how high they hold their torso. It is one of the more flexible positions for finding the exact rhythm that works.
items.makeItHotter: Try lowering from straight arms to forearms for a more grounded version, or lifting slightly higher through the chest and hips for a stronger angle. Hands can stay at the hips, waist, or lower back to help guide rhythm and support. A pillow under the knees or chest can also change the feel in a good way.
items.comfort: Knees, wrists, and lower back usually matter most here. A pillow under the knees can help a lot, and lowering to the forearms can reduce wrist strain. If the lower back starts to feel overworked, soften the arch and bring the torso closer to the bed.
items.bestFor: Classic rear-entry rhythm, easy angle adjustment, strong directional contact, a grounded versatile setup
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It is familiar, flexible, and easy to make feel exactly right. Many people like it because it is one of the most adjustable positions available, and because even small changes in posture or angle can completely shift the sensation.
lessIdealIf: Wrists or knees are sensitive
You want face-to-face connection
The mood is soft or emotionally tender
setupTips: Widen the knees for more stability
Lower to the forearms to reduce wrist strain
Place a pillow under the knees if needed
Soften the arch if the lower back tires
Adjust hip height before building pace
items.shortSummary: A classic rear-entry position that stays grounded, adaptable, and easy to personalize.

items.title: The Reflection
items.focus: A balanced, mirrored position where both partners meet each other with equal openness and support. Because the shape is nearly identical on both sides, it feels mutual, visually striking, and quietly intimate. There is a sense of symmetry here that makes the connection feel especially shared.
items.howTo: Both partners sit facing each other, leaning back into their hands for support. One leg stays extended while the other bends inward, creating a mirrored shape that brings the bodies into alignment. The position works best with slow adjustments through the hips and thighs until both partners find a comfortable, responsive angle.
items.benefits: Because both bodies are doing nearly the same thing, the position can feel unusually equal and synchronized. The support through the arms keeps the chest open, while the mirrored leg placement creates a strong sense of structure and mutual rhythm. It often feels playful, connected, and very intentional.
items.makeItHotter: Try shifting slightly closer together or changing how much each partner leans back to find the best angle. Small movements through the hips can make a bigger difference than you expect. Keeping the mirrored shape intact usually makes the whole position feel more elegant and connected.
items.comfort: A padded surface helps, since both partners are taking some weight through their hands and wrists. If the hamstrings or hips feel too tight, soften the bend in the extended leg or reduce the lean until the shape feels sustainable.
items.bestFor: Mirrored symmetry, mutual rhythm, a visually connected setup, playful intimate balance
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels unusually equal and intentional. Many people like it because the mirrored posture creates a sense of shared rhythm, and because the visual symmetry makes the connection feel more deliberate and synchronized.
lessIdealIf: Hamstring or hip tightness is present
You want strong directional momentum
The mood is more urgent than playful
setupTips: Use a padded surface to support the wrists
Soften the extended leg if the hamstrings are tight
Adjust the closeness before adjusting pace
Keep the mirrored shape rather than breaking it
Small hip shifts work better than big movements
items.shortSummary: A face-to-face mirrored position that feels balanced, mutual, and visually connected.

items.title: The Reign
items.focus: A classic upright riding position that feels confident, open, and fully self-directed. One partner stays grounded underneath while the other takes the lead from above, creating a shape that feels powerful without being complicated. It can be playful, sensual, or intensely focused depending on pace and posture.
items.howTo: One partner lies back while the other straddles them facing forward, settling into a stable seat above the hips. The partner on top can stay fully upright or lean slightly forward, using the lower partner's body, thighs, or the bed for balance as needed. The beauty of this position is how easy it is to personalize — small changes in posture, pace, and pressure can make it feel completely different.
items.benefits: Because the partner on top is leading most of the movement, this position often feels especially responsive and easy to control. A more upright posture can feel bold and open, while a slight lean can create a stronger sense of closeness and grounded pressure. The partner underneath stays involved through touch and support, but the overall energy comes from the person above setting the rhythm.
items.makeItHotter: Experiment with posture before speed. A slower grind can feel more intense than faster movement, especially when the angle is just right. Hands can stay on the thighs, chest, or bed for support, or move to the lower partner's body to make the whole position feel more connected and controlled.
items.comfort: This position can ask a lot from the top partner's thighs and knees over time, so comfort matters. A softer surface or pillow under the knees can help, and taking breaks by leaning forward or changing the rhythm can make it much easier to sustain. It usually feels best when the body stays active but not tense.
items.bestFor: Top-partner control, upright confidence, classic riding rhythm, a self-directed physical mood
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It is classic, reliable, and easy to personalize. Many people like it because it gives the partner on top direct control over pace and angle, and because the upright posture can feel both powerful and intimate depending on how close the bodies stay.
lessIdealIf: Top partner's thighs or knees are fatigued
You want rear-facing or face-away dynamics
The mood is more slow and tender than energized
setupTips: Try posture changes before speed changes
Use the lower partner's body or the bed for hand support
Place a pillow under the knees if needed
Slow grinding can feel more intense than fast movement
Stay active but not tense in the legs
items.shortSummary: A classic upright riding position that feels confident, easy to control, and fully self-directed.

items.title: The Exchange
items.focus: A mutual oral position built around giving and receiving at the same time. It feels playful, interactive, and highly responsive, with both partners actively involved throughout. Because attention is moving in both directions, the experience can feel exciting, immersive, and a little more playful than positions focused on only one person at a time.
items.howTo: Both partners arrange themselves so that each has comfortable access to the other's genitals, usually with one partner lying back and the other positioned above in the opposite direction, or with both partners side-lying if a softer version feels better. The key is not forcing perfect symmetry but finding a setup where breathing, neck position, and body weight all feel manageable. Start slowly, communicate clearly, and make small adjustments in height, angle, and leg placement until both partners can stay relaxed and engaged.
items.benefits: This position can feel especially interactive because both partners are participating at the same time. Some people love the sense of reciprocity and shared rhythm, while others find it more intense because there is a lot to pay attention to at once. It often works best when the goal is not perfection or constant motion, but curiosity, responsiveness, and a little back-and-forth play.
items.makeItHotter: Try changing the pace so one partner pauses while the other continues, then switch. That alternating rhythm can make the position feel more manageable and often more enjoyable than both trying to do everything at once. Small changes in angle, pressure, or where each body rests can also make a big difference.
items.comfort: Neck, shoulders, and jaw can tire more quickly here than people expect. Pillows under the head, hips, or upper body can help a lot, and side-lying variations are often easier to sustain than stacked ones. If either partner feels crowded, strained, or distracted by the setup, pause and reset rather than trying to push through discomfort.
items.bestFor: Simultaneous oral pleasure, playful reciprocity, interactive mutual focus, lighthearted shared intensity
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels reciprocal, immersive, and playful all at once. Many people like it because both partners are engaged at the same time, and because the alternating rhythm — rather than trying to do everything at once — makes it feel surprisingly manageable and fun.
goodFor: A more immersive and shared experience
lessIdealIf: Neck, jaw, or shoulder fatigue is present
You want to stay fully focused on giving or receiving
Either partner feels distracted by the setup
setupTips: Use pillows under the head, hips, or chest
Try a side-lying variation if the stacked version feels cramped
Alternate focus rather than both doing everything at once
Pause and reset if comfort breaks down
Communicate small adjustments freely
items.shortSummary: A mutual oral position built around reciprocity, playfulness, and staying interactive together.

items.title: The Solstice
items.focus: A supported legs-up position that creates a sharper, more exact angle while still keeping both partners close. With the lower partner's legs lifted and resting on the upper partner's shoulders, the shape feels open, held, and highly focused. It is less about constant motion and more about finding the angle that feels best and staying with it.
items.howTo: One partner lies back while the other comes in close above them, supporting their weight through the bed, floor, or surface beneath them. The lower partner lifts their legs so the feet or calves rest along the upper partner's shoulders, creating a more open angle through the hips. Start by settling into the shape slowly, then adjust depth, hip position, and torso lean until both partners find a version that feels steady and comfortable.
items.benefits: Because the legs are lifted, this position often creates a deeper and more concentrated sensation than flatter face-to-face positions. The bottom partner may feel more open and supported, while the top partner can stay close and responsive without needing a lot of dramatic movement. Once the angle is right, even small changes can feel strong.
items.makeItHotter: Bring the bodies closer together before increasing pace. A slower rhythm usually makes this one feel better than rushing it, especially because the position responds so strongly to small adjustments. Hands can stay planted for support or move to the thighs, hips, or waist once the angle feels secure.
items.comfort: Hip flexibility matters here. If the legs-on-shoulders version feels too intense, lower the legs slightly or rest them more loosely against the upper arms instead. A pillow under the hips can also soften the angle and make the shape easier to hold without strain.
items.bestFor: A deeper angle, supported leg lift, strong focused sensation, close face-to-face connection
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It creates a stronger, more focused angle quickly and clearly. Many people like it because once the legs are lifted the position becomes very responsive to small adjustments, and because the setup still keeps both partners facing each other.
lessIdealIf: Hip flexibility is limited
You want a softer or more relaxed position
The angle feels too intense at full lift
setupTips: Lower the legs to the arms if shoulders feel too intense
Place a pillow under the hips to soften the angle
Start slow and adjust depth before building pace
Bring the bodies closer before increasing movement
Small hip changes work better than large ones
items.shortSummary: A legs-up position that creates a deeper, more focused angle while keeping both partners close.

items.title: The Bridge
items.focus: A bold, backbend-inspired position that feels open, intense, and visually striking. One partner lifts into a bridge-like shape with the chest high and the body fully opened, while the other kneels between their legs to create a close, grounded connection. It feels exposed and powerful at the same time.
items.howTo: One partner presses into a deep backbend with the hands planted beneath or near the head, chest lifted, and hips elevated. The other partner kneels between their legs, staying close and using the position to guide rhythm and angle. This setup works best when the bridge itself feels stable first. Start by finding secure hand and foot placement, then build the rest of the position slowly.
items.benefits: Because the receiving partner is fully arched, this position creates a strong, open angle and a much more dramatic body line than lower, flatter setups. The bridge shape can make the body feel stretched, lifted, and highly exposed, while the kneeling partner gets a stable position to stay close and responsive. The intensity often comes from the posture itself as much as the movement.
items.makeItHotter: Once the bridge feels steady, even small changes in foot placement, hip height, or closeness can shift the sensation a lot. Staying slower often makes this one feel stronger because the angle is already doing so much of the work. The kneeling partner can stay close to make the shape feel more secure and more connected.
items.comfort: This one can be demanding on the wrists, shoulders, lower back, and thighs. It is usually best in shorter periods unless the partner in the bridge is already very comfortable in that shape. A padded surface under the hands and feet helps, and a modified version with the hips lowered slightly may feel better for many bodies.
items.bestFor: Full backbend energy, open dramatic angle, adventurous bold exposure, a visually striking intense setup
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels bold, exposed, and visually dramatic. Many people like it because the arch itself creates most of the intensity, making it feel powerful and adventurous even with slower or more controlled movement.
lessIdealIf: Wrists, shoulders, or lower back are sensitive
You are not comfortable in a backbend
You want something long-lasting or restful
setupTips: Find stable hand and foot placement before anything else
Use a padded surface under the hands
Keep the kneeling partner close for support
Slow pace lets the angle do the work
Reduce the arch if the lower back strains
items.shortSummary: A backbend position that feels open, dramatic, and adventurously intense.

items.title: The Conversation
items.focus: An open, responsive position built around access, feedback, and staying tuned in to each other. One partner stays lifted and supported while the other settles lower, creating a shape that feels intimate, exposed, and highly interactive. It is less about speed and more about touch, adjustment, and connection.
items.howTo: One partner braces themselves with their hands behind them, chest open and hips lifted slightly, while the other settles between their legs in a lower reclined position. The lower partner can use their hands to guide pressure, rhythm, or stimulation, making the position feel collaborative and easy to adapt. Small shifts in hip angle and distance will change the sensation quickly, so take time finding the best setup.
items.benefits: This position creates strong openness through the hips and torso while keeping both partners supported. Because the lower partner has easy access with their hands, it can feel especially attentive, customizable, and connected. The result is sensual, interactive, and very responsive to communication.
items.makeItHotter: Adjust how high the upper partner lifts through the chest and hips to change the angle. Hands can stay focused low for more direct stimulation or travel upward to add teasing touch through the thighs, stomach, or chest. Slowing down and staying deliberate usually makes this one feel even more intense.
items.comfort: The upper partner may want softer support under the hands or wrists, especially on a firm surface. If the neck or lower back feels strained, reduce the lean or place a cushion beneath the hips. This one works best when both partners can stay relaxed enough to keep responding to each other.
items.bestFor: Responsive touch and feedback, open access, collaborative adjustment, a sensual attentive mood
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels interactive, open, and easy to tune. Many people like it because the hand access and adjustable hip angle make it one of the more communicative setups available, and because it naturally rewards slowing down and paying attention.
lessIdealIf: Wrists or lower back tire quickly
You want something more physically intense or driven
The mood is more heated than attentive
setupTips: Support the upper partner's wrists or hands
Place a cushion under the hips if needed
Adjust hip height and distance as you go
Stay responsive rather than setting a fixed rhythm
Slow down to stay tuned in to each other
items.shortSummary: An open responsive position built around touch, easy access, and staying interactively connected.

items.title: The Horizon
items.focus: A face-away riding position that feels open, confident, and fully self-led. One partner stays grounded underneath while the other takes control from above, creating a shape that feels bold, exposed, and easy to customize. It can feel playful, intense, or steady depending on posture and pace.
items.howTo: One partner lies back while the other straddles them facing away, settling into a stable seat over the hips. The partner on top can stay upright for a more open, confident feel or lean slightly forward or back to explore different angles. Hands can rest on the thighs, bed, or the lower partner's legs for balance as the rhythm builds gradually.
items.benefits: Because the partner on top is setting most of the movement, this position often feels highly adjustable and very responsive to small posture changes. The face-away orientation can make it feel more private, more focused, and sometimes more intense than a face-to-face riding position. The lower partner stays involved through support and touch, while the upper partner controls the pace and pressure.
items.makeItHotter: Try changing the torso angle before changing speed. Sitting taller can feel more open and commanding, while a slight forward lean can create a stronger grounded sensation. Small hip shifts can make a big difference here, and slower movement often gives the top partner more control over what feels best.
items.comfort: This one can put extra work into the top partner's thighs and knees, so support matters. A pillow under the lower partner's hips can change the angle in a helpful way, and the top partner may want to use their hands more for balance to reduce tension in the legs. It usually feels best when the top partner stays active but not rigid.
items.bestFor: Face-away top control, open confident posture, self-directed rhythm, a more private or bold mood
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It puts the partner on top fully in charge while feeling open and self-directed. Many people like it because the face-away orientation creates a different kind of focus, and because the position is highly responsive to small posture changes.
goodFor: A more private or independent mood
lessIdealIf: The top partner's thighs or knees are tired
You want face-to-face connection
The mood is more tender than confident

Use the hands on the thighs or bed for balance
Place a pillow under the lower partner's hips if helpful
Stay active but not rigid in the legs
Slow movement often feels more controlled than fast
items.shortSummary: A face-away riding position that feels open, confident, and fully self-led.

items.title: The Overhang
items.focus: A grounded, edge-supported position that blends openness with control. One partner stays elevated and accessible while the other moves in close, creating a feeling that is direct, steady, and physically connected. It carries the intensity of an adventurous angle without feeling rushed or unstable.
items.howTo: One partner settles at the edge of the bed or surface with their weight supported through their hands, while the other kneels or positions close between their legs, guiding the lift at the hips and setting the pace. Keep the setup slow at first so both partners can find the most comfortable height and angle. Small adjustments make a big difference here, especially in the hips, knees, and hand placement.
items.benefits: The edge support creates a strong, open angle while still keeping both partners close. It can feel immersive and intense, but also more controlled than a fully lifted variation because the receiving partner still has a stable point of contact. There is a nice balance here between exposure, support, and closeness.
items.makeItHotter: Use the free hands to steady the hips, guide the rhythm, or add touch through the waist, thighs, or lower back. Slowing down and holding eye contact for a moment can make the position feel even more charged. A pillow or folded blanket under the hands can also soften the shape without changing the angle too much.
items.comfort: If the wrists or shoulders start to tire, place a pillow beneath the hands or raise the receiving partner slightly with extra cushion beneath the hips. The supporting partner may also want to come in closer to reduce strain and make the position feel more secure. This one works best when the body is supported, not forced.
items.bestFor: Open edge-supported angle, grounded intensity, close physical connection, direct supported rhythm
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It creates a strong open angle with more stability than a fully unsupported version. Many people like it because the edge support lets the receiving partner stay relaxed while the shape still feels intense and physically charged.
lessIdealIf: Wrists or shoulders tire quickly
The surface edge is too hard or unstable
You want something soft and fully reclined

Keep the setup slow until the angle feels right

Stay low enough that the support feels solid

items.shortSummary: A grounded edge-supported position with a strong open angle and close physical connection.

items.title: The Ascent
items.focus: A fully upright position that feels intense, close, and completely held. The lifted partner is wrapped in, supported, and suspended in the other's strength, which creates a charged mix of passion, security, and full-body contact. It feels immediate and consuming in a way that is hard to ignore.
items.howTo: One partner lifts the other upright so their legs wrap around the waist and their arms can settle around the shoulders or neck for balance and closeness. The supporting partner keeps a steady hold through the hips or thighs, using the wall, bed, or nearby surface if extra stability helps. Start slowly and let the lift feel secure before adding movement.
items.benefits: This position creates a strong sense of closeness because both bodies stay tightly connected from chest to hips. The height and lift can make it feel more intense and emotionally charged than more grounded positions, while the wrapped legs add a feeling of being fully gathered in. It is less about ease and more about total presence.
items.makeItHotter: Bring it against a wall for more support and less effort, or use slow pauses to make the closeness feel even more deliberate. Holding eye contact, kissing deeply, or adjusting the lift slightly higher or lower can completely change the energy. Sometimes the smallest shift in height creates the best rhythm.
items.comfort: This one asks more from the supporting partner's legs, core, and grip, so it usually works best in shorter bursts rather than long stretches. A wall behind the lifted partner or a surface close by can make it far more comfortable and sustainable. If either person feels unstable, reset right away and use more support.
items.bestFor: Full upright lift, total body closeness, passionate intensity, a strongly held and immersive moment
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels completely consuming and close. Many people like it because the full-body contact combined with the height and lift creates a sense of being fully gathered in, which can feel both passionate and deeply secure at the same time.
goodFor: A strong feeling of being held
lessIdealIf: Either partner has balance or strength concerns
You want something long-lasting or low-effort
The setup feels unstable without wall support

items.shortSummary: A full standing carry that feels intensely close, passionate, and completely held.

items.title: The Arch
items.focus: A dramatic, high-open position built around lift, arch, and exposure. The receiving partner is elevated through the hips with the legs lifted high, creating a shape that feels bold, physically intense, and visually striking. It has an adventurous energy, but when supported well, it can also feel steady and controlled.
items.howTo: One partner kneels or positions close while supporting the other securely at the hips or seat. The receiving partner leans back into their hands, keeping the chest open and the hips lifted, while the legs rise upward to create a sharper angle. Start by finding a stable hold and comfortable depth before increasing pace. This position works best when the support underneath feels strong and the receiving partner stays active through the arms and core.
items.benefits: Because the hips are elevated and the legs are lifted, this position creates a stronger, more direct angle than many lower, more grounded positions. It can feel especially intense and exposed, while the support at the hips helps keep it from feeling chaotic. The combination of lift, openness, and closeness makes it feel both bold and focused.
items.makeItHotter: A small change in leg height can noticeably shift the angle and sensation. Keeping the receiving partner closer to the supporting partner's body can make the shape feel more secure, while a slower pace can make the depth feel even more pronounced. Hands can stay planted for stability or soften slightly once the position feels steady.
items.comfort: This one can put more demand on the lower back, hamstrings, wrists, and hips than it first appears. A soft surface under the hands or extra support beneath the hips can help. If the lifted-leg version feels too intense, lower the legs slightly or reduce the arch until the body can stay supported without strain.
items.bestFor: Elevated hips, open dramatic angle, adventurous bold energy, a more visually striking intense setup
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels bold, open, and physically striking. Many people like it because the lift and elevation create a much stronger angle quickly, and because the visual drama of the shape adds to the overall intensity without needing extra effort.
lessIdealIf: Lower back, wrist, or hip sensitivity is present

items.shortSummary: A grounded face-to-face position that feels steady, secure, and emotionally anchored.

items.title: The Gaze
items.focus: A face-to-face position centered around eye contact and presence. The closeness feels immediate, vulnerable, and emotionally charged in a way that can be surprisingly intense even without a lot of movement. It is built for staying tuned in to each other rather than getting lost in momentum.
items.howTo: Both partners come into an upright or semi-upright face-to-face hold where the head, chest, and torso stay open enough for sustained eye contact. The position can be seated, standing, or supported against a surface, as long as the structure allows both people to stay close and present. This setup works best when the pace stays slow enough that the eye contact feels natural rather than forced.
items.benefits: Because the face remains such a central part of the experience, the position often feels emotionally exposing in the best way. It may feel more intimate, more intense, or more connected than positions that allow the attention to shift elsewhere. Even very small movements can feel powerful when the visual connection stays intact.
items.makeItHotter: Hold the eye contact a little longer, bring the faces closer, and let the pace stay slower than usual. Hands at the face, neck, waist, or lower back can deepen the feeling of connection. Sometimes the strongest version of this position comes from pausing rather than moving.
items.comfort: If the eye contact starts to feel too intense, soften it rather than abandoning the closeness completely. Looking at the mouth, cheek, or chest can keep the connection gentle. Physical support also matters here, because emotional intensity is easier to stay with when the body feels secure.
items.bestFor: Eye contact, emotional intensity, slower face-to-face connection, staying present
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels present, intimate, and emotionally honest. Many people like it because it turns closeness into something more than physical, without needing anything elaborate in the setup. It is especially good when emotional connection is part of what makes the moment powerful.
goodFor: A more vulnerable kind of connection
lessIdealIf: Either partner does not want sustained eye contact
The emotional intensity feels too high
You want something more playful or detached
setupTips: Choose a position with enough support to relax into
Keep the shoulders soft
Let the eye contact come and go naturally
Slow the pace down
Use touch at the face or waist to stay connected
items.shortSummary: A face-to-face position built around eye contact, presence, and slow emotional intensity.

items.title: The Orbit
items.focus: A face-to-face position that emphasizes circular, rolling, or spiraling movement instead of straight-on rhythm. It feels fluid, sensual, and responsive, with the energy coming from motion quality more than force. The whole experience can feel softer, more exploratory, and more body-aware.
items.howTo: Both partners settle into a face-to-face setup with enough support and closeness to keep the upper body connected while the hips move more freely. Rather than pushing in a direct line, the rhythm comes from circling, rolling, or tracing smaller arcs that let both bodies respond to the motion. This works best when the pace stays slow enough that the body can actually follow the curve.
items.benefits: Because the movement is less linear, the sensation often feels more nuanced and more continuously responsive. The position may feel less mechanical and more like a shared flow, especially when both partners are tuned into the same rhythm. It often creates a sensual, almost hypnotic quality.
items.makeItHotter: Make the circles smaller and slower instead of bigger and faster. A hand at the hip, waist, thigh, or lower back can help guide the motion and keep the bodies close. Once the rhythm clicks, even tiny circles can feel strong and very connected.
items.comfort: This position usually works best when the base is secure. If too much energy is spent trying to hold the body upright, the circular motion can feel awkward instead of fluid. Use pillows, a backrest, or a surface for support if needed so the hips can stay relaxed enough to move naturally.
items.bestFor: Circular movement, exploring rhythm, more sensual flow, responsive body connection
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels sensual, exploratory, and less scripted than more direct movement. Many people like it because it helps them focus on rhythm, body awareness, and connection rather than performance or pace. It can be especially good for discovering what feels best through slower movement.
lessIdealIf: You want straightforward, high-momentum movement
The base feels unstable
Either partner finds circular motion awkward
setupTips: Support the upper body first
Start with smaller circles
Keep the torso close
Use the hands to guide the hips
Stay slow until the rhythm feels natural
items.shortSummary: A face-to-face position built around circular movement, sensual flow, and shared rhythm.

items.title: The Pulse
items.focus: A deeply focused position built around tiny, synchronized movement rather than speed or range. It feels quiet, deliberate, and surprisingly intense because so little motion is needed. The energy comes from staying close enough to feel every shift, pause, and response.
items.howTo: Both partners settle into a supported face-to-face setup where the body can stay relaxed and closely aligned. Instead of using large movement, the rhythm comes from tiny pulses, gentle rocking, or subtle pressure changes that both bodies can feel clearly. This position works best when neither person rushes and the pace stays controlled enough to notice the smallest details.
items.benefits: Because the movement stays so contained, the sensation often feels concentrated and immediate. Small adjustments can feel much stronger than expected, and the shared rhythm can create a strong sense of intimacy and attunement. It often feels more intense than it looks.
items.makeItHotter: Slow down even more and make the movement smaller instead of bigger. Keep the chest, hips, or thighs close enough that the whole body stays involved. A hand at the lower back, thigh, or waist can help guide the pulse and make the rhythm feel even more connected.
items.comfort: This position usually feels best when the setup is highly supported, since the goal is subtlety rather than effort. If either partner is straining to hold posture, add support first. The less work the body has to do, the easier it is to feel the pulse clearly.
items.bestFor: Small movement, slow intensity, staying present, finding a steady shared rhythm
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels intimate, controlled, and deeply responsive. Many people like it because it proves that intensity does not always come from speed, and because the smallness of the movement makes the connection feel more mutual and tuned in.
lessIdealIf: You want big movement or high energy
The position feels under-supported
Either partner has trouble slowing down into subtle motion
setupTips: Start with a very stable base
Make the movement smaller, not faster
Keep the torso close
Use touch to guide the rhythm
Let pauses be part of the experience
items.shortSummary: A slow, face-to-face position built around tiny pulses and surprisingly intense minimal movement.

items.title: The Clasp
items.focus: A face-to-face position where linked hands or entwined arms become part of the intimacy. It feels mutual, intentional, and emotionally close, with the sense that both partners are actively holding each other in the moment. The added hand connection makes the whole setup feel more deliberate and shared.
items.howTo: Both partners come into a face-to-face position with enough openness through the chest and shoulders to let the hands meet naturally. The hands can clasp between the bodies, above the chest, or around the shoulders and forearms depending on what feels most connected. This position works best when the hand or arm contact feels supportive instead of tense, adding to the rhythm rather than distracting from it.
items.benefits: Because the hands stay involved, the position often feels more emotionally mutual and less physically separate. The touch through the palms, fingers, or forearms can make the whole experience feel steadier and more intentional. It often creates a stronger sense of being in it together.
items.makeItHotter: Tighten the handhold slightly, shift the clasp from palms to fingers, or let the arms wrap more fully as the rhythm deepens. Eye contact can make this one feel especially intimate. Often, the more relaxed the hand connection is, the more meaningful it feels.
items.comfort: Shoulders, wrists, and forearms should stay soft. If the handholding starts to feel strained, change the angle or move the clasp to a more comfortable place rather than dropping it entirely. This one works best when the hands feel like part of the support, not extra effort.
items.bestFor: Mutuality, hand-holding, emotional closeness, a more intentional connected feel
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels intentional, emotionally close, and distinctly mutual. Many people like it because hand contact changes the tone of the position, making it feel less routine and more like a shared experience. It is especially good when emotional connection matters as much as physical closeness.
lessIdealIf: Either partner wants more freedom through the arms
Shoulder or wrist comfort is limited
The mood is more athletic or highly physical
setupTips: Let the handhold be soft, not rigid
Find a clasp position that feels natural
Keep the shoulders relaxed
Use the hands to guide pace gently
Adjust the arms before adjusting speed
items.shortSummary: A face-to-face position where clasped hands add a mutual, intentional feeling of connection.

items.title: The Vow
items.focus: A very still, close face-to-face position that feels reverent, emotionally charged, and almost ceremonial in its intimacy. It is built around presence first, not pace. The closeness can feel powerful even before movement begins, simply because both people stay there long enough to fully feel it.
items.howTo: Both partners settle into a supported face-to-face hold that allows the body to stay close without rushing into motion. The chest, hips, arms, and face all stay near enough that the stillness itself becomes part of the experience. This position works best when both partners let the pause matter instead of treating it as a transition to get through.
items.benefits: Because the position begins in stillness, the sensation often feels emotionally heightened and deeply present. Breath, pressure, warmth, and anticipation all become more noticeable. When movement does happen, even a very small shift can feel amplified by how much closeness is already there.
items.makeItHotter: Stay still longer. Keep the chest close, hold eye contact if it feels good, and let the hands rest with intention at the face, waist, lower back, or thighs. A slower beginning often makes the rest of the experience feel far more intense and connected.
items.comfort: This position needs support, because stillness is harder to sustain when the body is working too hard. Pillows, a headboard, or a wall can help the body relax enough to stay fully present. If the stillness starts to feel strained instead of intimate, adjust the support before continuing.
items.bestFor: Stillness, emotional intensity, deep presence, lingering closeness before movement builds
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels deeply intimate and emotionally rich. Many people like it because it creates a sense of significance and presence that more active positions can sometimes skip over. It is especially powerful when the mood is tender, intense, or deeply connected.
lessIdealIf: Either partner feels uncomfortable with stillness
The mood is playful or impatient
The setup is under-supported
setupTips: Choose a highly supported position
Let the pause be intentional
Keep the body close but breathable
Use touch sparingly and meaningfully
Add movement only when it feels natural
items.shortSummary: A very still face-to-face position where presence, anticipation, and closeness lead the experience.

items.title: The Offering
items.focus: A receiving-focused position where one partner relaxes fully while the other gives them complete attention. It feels open, intentional, and cared for, with the whole setup designed to make it easier to receive without needing to perform or hold tension. The energy is attentive, focused, and quietly intimate.
items.howTo: One partner reclines or lies back in a comfortable, supported position with the hips, legs, and torso arranged to feel open without strain. The other partner settles in close enough to give focused attention, using hands, mouth, or touch in whatever way fits the moment and the bodies involved. This setup works best when comfort is established first, so the receiving partner can soften and the giving partner can stay responsive.
items.benefits: Because the receiving partner is not asked to do much physically, the sensation often feels more immersive and easier to relax into. The attentive focus from the other partner can make the experience feel especially cared for and intentional. It tends to feel less rushed and more centered on responsiveness and comfort.
items.makeItHotter: Add support under the hips, lower back, knees, or head to refine the angle and make the body feel even more open. Slowing down usually deepens this one more than trying to add intensity too quickly. A hand at the thigh, stomach, waist, or face can make the whole position feel even more connected and attentive.
items.comfort: Support matters a lot here. Pillows or cushions can help the receiving partner relax fully and can also improve the angle for the giving partner. If the giving partner's neck, jaw, shoulders, or wrists start to strain, pause and adjust rather than pushing through discomfort.
items.bestFor: Centering one partner's pleasure, receiving fully, relaxed attentiveness, a supported open setup
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels focused, generous, and deeply attentive. Many people like it because it centers one person's pleasure in a way that feels relaxing rather than performative. It can be especially good when the mood is intimate, patient, and fully present.
lessIdealIf: Either partner wants something faster or more mutual in the moment
Support is limited
Neck, jaw, or wrist comfort is already compromised
setupTips: Support the hips and head first
Keep the receiving body open but relaxed
Let comfort shape the angle
Pause early if strain builds
Stay attentive and responsive instead of rushing
items.shortSummary: A supported receiving-focused position designed to center comfort, openness, and full attention.

items.title: The Spark
items.focus: A quick-transition position that feels impulsive, lively, and full of chemistry. It often begins standing or mid-movement and shifts naturally onto a bed, couch, counter, or other surface without losing the energy of the moment. The excitement comes from the transition itself — that feeling of not overplanning, just following the spark.
items.howTo: Both partners begin upright or in motion, then move into a more supported setup on the nearest comfortable surface. One partner may lean, sit, or brace first while the other follows in close, keeping the pace playful rather than perfectly choreographed. The key to this position is not precision but responsiveness: let the movement unfold quickly, then slow just enough to make the shape feel secure.
items.benefits: Because this one is built around momentum, it often feels more exciting and charged than positions that start already settled. The transition itself can heighten the mood, making the body feel more awake, playful, and engaged. Once the bodies land, the position often carries that same spontaneous energy even if the pace slows.
items.makeItHotter: Let the transition happen with confidence, but not recklessness. A hand at the waist, thigh, or lower back can make the movement feel smoother and more connected. Once the shape is established, a brief pause can make the chemistry feel even stronger before things continue.
items.comfort: Choose a surface that is stable and close enough to move into without strain. This one works best when the spontaneity feels exciting, not unstable. If something lands awkwardly, laugh, reset, and try again instead of forcing the moment.
items.bestFor: Spontaneous moments, quick transitions, playful chemistry, turning impulse into momentum
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels alive, immediate, and genuinely playful. Many people like it because it turns the transition into part of the intimacy instead of just a step between positions. It can make the whole experience feel more flirtatious and chemistry-driven.
lessIdealIf: The space feels cramped or unstable
One or both partners want a very slow, settled start
Balance or mobility feels limited in the moment
setupTips: Choose the surface before you need it
Keep the first movement simple
Slow down once you land
Use hands to guide and stabilize
Let playful energy stay part of the experience
items.shortSummary: A spontaneous transition position that turns upright chemistry into playful, surface-based momentum.

items.title: The Surge
items.focus: A supported position with the hips raised to create a stronger, more charged angle. It feels immediate, high-energy, and physically focused, but still grounded enough to stay controlled. There is a sense of lift and intensity here that makes the whole position feel more energized from the start.
items.howTo: One partner lies back with the hips elevated on a pillow, wedge, or folded blanket while the other kneels or positions close in front of them. The raised hips sharpen the angle, so it helps to begin slowly and let both bodies adjust before increasing pace. Hands can stay at the hips, thighs, or surface for support and control.
items.benefits: Because the hips are lifted, the contact often feels stronger and more direct than flatter, more relaxed positions. The support underneath can make the position feel more sustainable even as the intensity increases. It often creates a very responsive setup where even small changes in height or posture make a noticeable difference.
items.makeItHotter: Add or remove a little height under the hips until the angle feels right. A steadier pace often feels stronger here than an overly rushed one, because the position already does a lot of the work. Hands guiding the hips or thighs can make the movement feel even more intentional and controlled.
items.comfort: Too much height can make the angle feel sharp instead of supported, so build gradually. A softer support under the hips usually feels better than something too firm or narrow. If the lower back feels strained, reduce the lift and bring the bodies slightly closer together.
items.bestFor: A stronger angle, elevated hips, energized rhythm, supported intensity
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels intense without needing a complicated setup. Many people like it because the elevated angle changes the sensation quickly and clearly, while the pillow or wedge keeps the body supported enough to stay engaged. It is a good blend of energy and structure.
goodFor: Small setup changes with big payoff
lessIdealIf: Lower-back sensitivity is already present
You want something soft, cuddly, or low-angle
The support under the hips feels unstable
setupTips: Start with a smaller lift than you think
Use something wide and supportive under the hips
Adjust height before pace
Keep the knees and lower back comfortable
Let the setup settle before building speed
items.shortSummary: A hips-elevated supported position that creates a stronger angle and a more energized feel.

items.title: The Flip
items.focus: A fluid reversal position where one partner transitions from underneath to above, or from leading less to leading more, without fully breaking the rhythm. It feels playful, dynamic, and a little teasing because the power shift becomes part of the excitement. The position is less about the final shape and more about the flip itself.
items.howTo: Begin in any stable position where both partners can move without losing contact, then let one partner guide a smooth reversal into a new lead position. This might mean rolling, sitting up, shifting onto a surface, or moving from above to below in one connected sequence. The goal is not a perfect acrobatic switch, but a confident, responsive handoff that keeps the energy alive.
items.benefits: Because the transition is built into the experience, the position often feels more playful and alive than static setups. The moment of switching control can make the whole rhythm feel new again. It often creates a strong sense of interaction and mutual participation.
items.makeItHotter: Slow the transition down enough to enjoy it instead of rushing through it. Hands at the waist, hips, thighs, or shoulders can help guide the movement and make it feel smooth rather than clumsy. Eye contact, laughter, or a brief pause once the switch is complete can make the reversal feel even more charged.
items.comfort: This one works best when the starting position is stable and both partners know where the weight is going. Use the bed, couch, or nearby surface to help with the shift if needed. If the movement starts to feel awkward, reset instead of forcing the flip.
items.bestFor: Changing who leads, mid-flow transitions, playful reversals, keeping the energy dynamic
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It keeps things lively and makes the shift in control part of the intimacy. Many people like it because it breaks routine without requiring a brand new position every time. It can feel flirtatious, equal, and energizing all at once.
lessIdealIf: The starting position is unstable
Either partner wants something very still or predictable
Balance or mobility is limited in the moment
setupTips: Start from a stable position
Guide the weight with the hands
Don't rush the switch
Use the surface for support
Reset if the transition feels off
items.shortSummary: A playful reversal position where the lead changes mid-flow without breaking the rhythm.

items.title: The Cascade
items.focus: Less a single position than a flowing sequence, The Cascade is all about movement from one shape into the next. It feels exploratory, spirited, and alive, with the excitement coming from not staying anywhere too long. The energy is playful and intuitive rather than rigid or scripted.
items.howTo: Start in any stable position, then let the bodies move naturally into another as comfort, rhythm, and curiosity guide the session. This might mean rolling from face-to-face into side-lying, shifting from a surface to the bed, or moving from a seated setup into something more reclined. The point is not to collect positions, but to let the movement between them feel smooth and connected.
items.benefits: Because the body does not settle into just one shape, the whole experience often feels more adventurous and less repetitive. The transitions can keep the energy high and make the session feel responsive to mood, comfort, and chemistry. It often creates a stronger sense of play and discovery.
items.makeItHotter: Let one position naturally suggest the next instead of deciding everything in advance. A hand guiding the waist, thigh, or lower back can help each transition feel more fluid. If something feels especially good, linger there for a while before letting the movement continue.
items.comfort: Flow works best when the environment is supportive enough to move around safely. Pillows can stay nearby, and surfaces should be stable and easy to transition onto or off of. If one transition feels awkward, pause and re-center instead of pushing forward just to keep the sequence going.
items.bestFor: Moving through multiple positions, playful exploration, variety, staying fluid instead of fixed
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels playful, freeing, and less formulaic. Many people like it because it allows the session to evolve naturally instead of staying locked into one position. It can be especially good when both people want variety without losing connection.
goodFor: Turning transitions into part of the fun
lessIdealIf: You want one stable, sustained setup
The space is too cramped to move easily
Either partner feels overwhelmed by too much change
setupTips: Keep the space clear and supportive
Let transitions happen naturally
Don't rush just to move on
Use touch to guide the next shift
Linger when something feels especially good
items.shortSummary: A flowing sequence of position changes that keeps the energy playful, varied, and connected.

items.title: The Chase
items.focus: A bent-over-surface position that feels direct, energized, and hard to ignore. One partner braces against a bed, counter, couch, or other surface while the other comes in close from behind, creating a shape that feels immediate and full of momentum. It carries a stronger, more urgent energy than slower rear-entry positions.
items.howTo: One partner leans over a stable surface, supporting themselves through the hands, forearms, or torso, while the other stands or kneels close behind. The height of the surface will shape the angle, so it helps to adjust posture before building pace. This position works best when the front partner feels supported enough to stay relaxed and the back partner stays close enough that the shape feels connected rather than stretched.
items.benefits: Because the body is angled forward and supported, the contact can feel more direct and energized than lower or more reclined positions. The partner in front may feel open and braced, while the partner behind often feels strong directional control over rhythm and pace. It tends to feel bold, physical, and fast-building.
items.makeItHotter: Bring the bodies closer to reduce the sense of reaching and increase the feeling of pressure and connection. A hand at the hip, waist, thigh, or lower back can make the rhythm feel more guided and more intentional. Changing how much the front partner leans can shift the angle dramatically.
items.comfort: The front partner's wrists, lower back, and stance matter most here. A softer bend in the knees or lowering from hands to forearms can reduce strain, and the surface should feel sturdy enough to lean into confidently. If the body starts to feel overextended, reduce the reach before changing anything else.
items.bestFor: Bent-over surface play, momentum, strong angles, a more urgent and driven mood
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels classic, accessible, and instantly charged. Many people like it because it creates strong energy without needing a complex setup, and because the surface support makes it easier to settle into than a fully unsupported standing position.
lessIdealIf: The surface is unstable or too high
Wrists or lower back are already tired
You want something soft, face-to-face, or emotionally gentle
setupTips: Choose a sturdy surface
Lower to the forearms if needed
Keep a slight bend in the knees
Adjust the lean before increasing pace
Stay close enough that the position feels supported
items.shortSummary: A bent-over-surface position with strong angles, easy support, and a more urgent rear-entry feel.

items.title: The Current
items.focus: A high-energy position built around strong directional movement and momentum. It feels driven, purposeful, and powerful, like both bodies are working with force instead of simply settling into rhythm. The energy is more athletic and intense than playful or soft.
items.howTo: Both partners move into a position that allows for stronger forward rhythm, whether kneeling, standing, or supported against a surface. The key is not the exact shape so much as the feeling of a clear directional line through the body. This position works best when the base feels steady and both partners can match the pace without losing control.
items.benefits: Because the rhythm is stronger and more directional, the sensation often feels more immediate and forceful than circular or slower positions. The body may feel more activated, especially through the legs, hips, and core. It often creates a sense of momentum that carries the whole experience forward.
items.makeItHotter: Focus on the line of movement instead of trying to add extra motion in every direction. A hand at the hips, waist, thigh, or surface can help guide the pace and make it feel even more intentional. Staying close enough that the movement feels connected keeps the power from turning into strain.
items.comfort: This position can ask more from the body than softer setups, especially in the knees, thighs, lower back, and core. Support matters, and so does pacing. If the movement starts to feel sloppy or overworked, reduce speed and rebuild the rhythm from control.
items.bestFor: Stronger momentum, more athletic movement, powerful rhythm, a driven physical mood
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels powerful, clear, and intensely physical. Many people like it because it creates a stronger sense of momentum and drive without necessarily needing a complicated or highly acrobatic position. It is often a good fit for moments that feel more heated and body-led.
lessIdealIf: You want tenderness, stillness, or a slower emotional tone
Body fatigue is already present
The position base feels unstable
setupTips: Start from a stable base
Keep the movement directional, not chaotic
Stay close enough for control
Pace with the body, not against it
Slow down before fatigue turns to strain
items.shortSummary: A momentum-based position with stronger directional movement and a more athletic, heated feel.

items.title: The Switch
items.focus: A collaborative position built around shifting lead and rhythm back and forth. It feels responsive, interactive, and playful, with neither partner locked into one role for too long. The whole mood is shaped by exchange and flexibility rather than control staying in one place.
items.howTo: Begin in a position where both partners can adjust posture, angle, or pace without fully resetting. One partner may take the lead for a while, then soften or reposition so the other can naturally guide the next phase. This works best when both people stay attentive to the shifts, letting the transition feel shared rather than abrupt.
items.benefits: Because the rhythm and control move between partners, the experience often feels lively and mutual. The changes can make even familiar positions feel new again, and the exchange itself can create a lot of chemistry. It often feels equal, engaged, and highly interactive.
items.makeItHotter: Let the change in lead happen through touch and posture as much as through big position shifts. A hand guiding the waist, thighs, hips, or shoulders can signal the transition without breaking the mood. The smoother the handoff, the more playful and connected the whole position feels.
items.comfort: This position works best when both partners are comfortable responding instead of trying to control everything at once. If the shifts start to feel confusing, slow down and simplify. The goal is easy exchange, not constant change for its own sake.
items.bestFor: Changing who leads, back-and-forth dynamics, playful collaboration, easy variation without stopping
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels mutual, dynamic, and less predictable. Many people like it because it opens space for both partners to shape the experience without needing to fully stop and restart. It can make the session feel more shared and more alive.
lessIdealIf: One partner strongly prefers a fixed role
The pace feels too confusing or inconsistent
You want stillness or sustained focus in one shape
setupTips: Choose a flexible starting position
Use hands and body language to guide the shift
Slow down when changing lead
Keep the exchange simple at first
Let collaboration shape the rhythm
items.shortSummary: A collaborative position built around changing who leads without breaking the flow.

items.title: The Glide
items.focus: A movement-led position built around smoothness instead of pressure or speed. It feels fluid, sensual, and easy to stay inside, with the pleasure coming from continuous flow rather than forceful rhythm. The mood is lighter, more responsive, and less rigid than more driven positions.
items.howTo: Choose a supported position where the hips and torso can move freely without the body having to work too hard to stay upright. The focus is on gliding, rolling, or smoothly shifting pressure rather than thrusting in a straight line. This works best when the movement stays relaxed enough that the body can follow it naturally.
items.benefits: Because the motion stays smooth and continuous, the sensation often feels more sensual and connected than abrupt or directional. It can create a feeling of easy momentum that keeps building without becoming harsh. The whole body may feel more involved because the rhythm is less segmented.
items.makeItHotter: Reduce effort and increase smoothness. A hand at the waist, thigh, lower back, or hip can help keep the motion connected and guide the flow. Often, the less you force this one, the better it feels.
items.comfort: This position works best when the body is supported enough to relax into movement. If the setup feels too upright or effortful, the flow can turn choppy. Add pillows, back support, or a softer surface if needed so the rhythm can stay easy.
items.bestFor: Smooth motion, sensual rhythm, easy transitions, staying fluid instead of forceful
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It feels sensual, less performative, and easier to sink into. Many people like it because it encourages rhythm without pressure and makes the experience feel more fluid than mechanical. It is especially good when the goal is pleasure through movement quality rather than intensity alone.
lessIdealIf: You want stronger impact or more athletic energy
The position is too unsupported to stay fluid
Either partner prefers a more direct rhythm
setupTips: Choose a position with easy hip freedom
Support the upper body enough to relax
Start slower than you think
Follow the motion instead of forcing it
Keep the rhythm continuous, not sharp
items.shortSummary: A movement-led position built around smooth, sensual rhythm and continuous flow.

items.title: The Driftwood
items.focus: A flatter, more relaxed side or rear variation that feels grounded, easy, and body-close. It carries some of the same behind-the-body energy as more upright rear-entry positions, but softens it into something slower and more settled. The mood is less driven and more about staying close to the surface and to each other.
items.howTo: Both partners stay low to the bed or surface, with one partner positioned slightly behind or angled off to the side of the other. The body line can stay elongated or slightly curled, depending on comfort and the angle that works best. This position works best when the setup feels easy to hold, with no one straining to keep the shape.
items.benefits: Because the bodies stay low and supported, the sensation often feels steadier and less intense in an athletic way than upright rear positions. The contact can feel fuller and more continuous, especially through the hips, thighs, and torso. It often creates a warm, grounded feeling that is easy to settle into.
items.makeItHotter: Bring the torso closer, adjust the top leg or hip angle slightly, and let the movement stay smooth rather than forceful. A hand at the waist, thigh, stomach, or lower back can make the position feel more connected. Small angle changes matter more here than speed.
items.comfort: Pillows under the hips, knees, or chest can improve comfort and alignment. If the position starts to feel too flat, a slight bend in one leg can open the angle without losing the relaxed shape. This one works best when the body stays soft and supported.
items.bestFor: A softer behind-the-body dynamic, low-effort closeness, lying-down support, slower steady rhythm
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It keeps a behind-the-body dynamic without requiring a lot of effort or leverage. Many people like it because it feels accessible, body-close, and easier to stay in than higher-effort rear-entry options. It is especially good when the mood is warm, steady, and unhurried.
lessIdealIf: You want a strong upright angle
You want fast momentum or a lot of range
The position feels too flat without support
setupTips: Use pillows under the hips or knees if needed
Stay low and body-close
Bend one leg slightly to refine the angle
Keep the torso relaxed
Focus on ease before pace
items.shortSummary: A low, grounded side or rear variation that keeps the behind-the-body dynamic soft, supported, and easy to hold.

items.title: The Plunge
items.focus: A hips-lifted position designed to create a deeper, more concentrated angle. It feels focused, intense, and highly responsive, with the elevation doing much of the work. The mood is deliberate and physical, but still controlled when the setup is well supported.
items.howTo: One partner positions with the hips elevated on a wedge, pillow stack, or folded blankets while the other kneels or comes in close from behind. The support beneath the hips creates a more pronounced angle, so it helps to begin slowly and let the body adjust before building rhythm. This position works best when the lift feels stable and broad, not sharp or precarious.
items.benefits: Because the hips are raised high, the contact often feels deeper and more direct than in flatter rear-entry setups. The support can make the position feel intense without requiring constant muscular effort from the partner being supported. Small changes in height, lean, or knee placement can noticeably change the sensation.
items.makeItHotter: Add height gradually until the angle feels strong but sustainable. Staying slower often makes this one feel more intense than rushing, because the setup already amplifies sensation. Hands at the hips, waist, or thighs can help guide the rhythm and keep the position feeling controlled.
items.comfort: Too much elevation can make the angle feel harsh rather than supported. Use something wide and steady under the hips, and reduce the lift if the lower back, hips, or knees start to strain. This one works best when the body feels held by the setup, not forced into it.
items.bestFor: A sharper angle, elevated support, stronger sensation, focused intensity
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It creates a strong angle quickly and clearly. Many people like it because the support does so much of the structural work, making the position feel intense without needing to be overly athletic. It is especially good when you want something focused and physical.
lessIdealIf: Lower-back sensitivity is already present
The support under the hips feels unstable
You want something soft, cuddly, or low-angle

Use a broad, stable support

items.shortSummary: A hips-elevated rear-entry position that creates a sharper angle and stronger, more focused intensity.

items.title: The Lean
items.focus: A supported bent-forward position that feels direct, stable, and easy to get into. One partner leans into a bed, counter, bench, or other surface while the other comes in close from behind, creating a shape that feels structured without being overly intense. It is practical, adaptable, and easy to personalize.
items.howTo: One partner braces against a stable surface using the hands, forearms, elbows, or torso, while the other stands or kneels behind them. The amount of lean can vary — more upright for a gentler angle, lower for a stronger one. This position works best when the front partner has enough support to relax into the surface instead of holding all the weight alone.
items.benefits: Because the surface provides structure, the position often feels steadier and more accessible than unsupported rear-entry positions. The front partner can adjust the angle simply by changing how much they lean, while the partner behind can stay close and connected. It tends to feel reliable, adaptable, and easy to settle into.
items.makeItHotter: Lower from hands to forearms, widen the stance slightly, or bring the bodies closer to increase the sense of support and pressure. A hand at the waist, hip, or thigh can make the rhythm feel more guided. Small posture changes often shift the feel more than speed does.
items.comfort: Wrist, shoulder, and lower-back comfort matter most here. A softer bend in the knees and a stable surface height can help a lot. If the body starts to feel overextended, reduce the lean before trying anything else.
items.bestFor: Easy setup, bent-forward support, stable rear-entry angles, a simpler surface-based option
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It is simple, flexible, and easy to adapt to different spaces. Many people like it because it offers the directness of a behind-the-body position with more built-in support than a fully freestanding version. It is also easy to modify without losing the core shape.
lessIdealIf: The surface is unstable
Wrists or lower back are already tired
You want a very soft, face-to-face mood

Keep a slight bend in the knees

items.title: The Overturn
items.focus: A reversal position where the partner who was previously underneath or receiving rises up and takes the lead. It feels dynamic, exciting, and symbolically charged because the whole energy of the moment shifts along with the physical arrangement. The appeal is as much in the change itself as in the final shape.
items.howTo: Start from any position where the transition into a lead-taking role can happen smoothly, then let the partner shifting upward guide the movement into the new setup. This might involve sitting up, rotating, rolling, or repositioning onto a surface while maintaining connection. The position works best when the reversal feels deliberate and confident rather than rushed.
items.benefits: Because the dynamic changes so clearly, the position often feels fresh and energizing even if the movement is simple. The partner taking over may feel more powerful, open, or self-directed, while the other partner may feel the excitement of being reoriented into a different role. It tends to add a strong spark to the mood.
items.makeItHotter: Slow the transition just enough to let the reversal register. Hands at the hips, waist, shoulders, or thighs can guide the movement and reinforce the new lead. Once the switch is complete, a brief pause can make the role change feel even more intentional.
items.comfort: This works best when the starting position is stable and both partners know where the weight is going. Use the bed, couch, or a nearby surface if needed to make the transition smoother. If the shift feels clumsy or forced, reset and try again more simply.
items.bestFor: Reversing the dynamic, taking the lead mid-flow, a symbolic power shift, keeping the energy charged
items.whyPeopleLikeIt: It changes the energy quickly and memorably. Many people like it because it brings in novelty and a sense of power exchange without needing a completely different style of position. It can make familiar movement feel new again.
goodFor: Making a familiar rhythm feel new
lessIdealIf: The transition feels unstable
One partner strongly prefers a fixed role
The mood is more about stillness or steadiness
